1: Simeon's allotment is embedded within Judah's territory — the only tribe without a geographically distinct territory. This unusual arrangement has a historical background: Jacob's deathbed curse on Simeon and Levi for the Shechem massacre (Gen 34; Genesis 49:5-7 — 'I will scatter them in Jacob and disperse them in Israel') is fulfilled here: Levi is scattered as a landless priestly tribe, and Simeon is absorbed within Judah's territory. The curse shapes the allotment.
2: Simeon's cities begin with Beersheba — the patriarchal boundary site (Genesis 21:31-33; Joshua 26:33), the southern limit of the promised land ('from Dan to Beersheba'). Simeon's territory at the very south of Canaan placed the tribe in the most exposed position, facing Edomite and nomadic pressure; the combination of exposure and absorption into Judah explains why Simeon essentially disappears as a distinct entity after the early monarchy. 1 Chronicles 4:24-43 preserves a late tradition of Simeonite expansion into the Negev and Seir, suggesting some Simeonites maintained a separate tribal identity by migrating further south and east.
3: Hazar-shual, Balah, Azem — Negev frontier sites. The same cities appear in Judah's Negev list (Joshua 15:28-29), confirming the overlap between Simeon's allotment and Judah's claimed territory. The administrative solution was pragmatic: Simeon was given title to cities within Judah's over-large claim, effectively forming an enclave with no border of its own.
4: Eltolad, Bethul, Hormah — again overlapping with Judah's city list (Joshua 15:30). The three-way overlap (Simeon's list / Judah's list / Simeon within Judah's boundary) is not an editorial inconsistency but a reflection of a real administrative situation: Simeon held specific cities as its allotment while Judah held the surrounding territory and overall jurisdiction.
5: Ziklag — mentioned in both Simeon's and Judah's lists. This is the Philistine-controlled city that Achish gave David as his residence during the Saul years (1 Samuel 27:6), with the narrator noting 'it has belonged to the kings of Judah to this day.' The Davidic royal claim to Ziklag — bypassing both Simeon's original title and Philistine occupation — illustrates how the allotment system interacted with military conquest and royal grant in the later monarchy.
6: Beth-lebaoth, Sharuhen — the list approaches the Gaza corridor and Philistine territory. Sharuhen (Tell el-Farah South) is archaeologically significant as an Egyptian administrative site in the Negev; its inclusion in Simeon's allotment acknowledges a region that was politically contested between Egypt, Philistia, and Israel throughout the early Iron Age.
7: Ain, Rimmon, Ether, Ashan — four cities closing the first group. Rimmon appears in Nehemiah 11:29 as a post-exilic settlement, confirming that Simeonite/southern Judean towns were still recognized as distinct settlements even after the Babylonian exile and return.
8: All the surrounding villages including Baalath-beer/Ramah of the Negev. The phrase 'all the villages surrounding these cities as far as Baalath-beer' indicates that the allotment includes both the named cities and their agricultural hinterlands. Baalath-beer ('mistress of the well') preserves a Canaanite female deity's name; the southern Negev place-names reflect the Canaanite, Amalekite, and nomadic cultures that had inhabited this zone for centuries before Israel.
9: 'The inheritance of Simeon was taken from the allotment of Judah, because Judah's share was too large for them.' This administrative note is unusual — it acknowledges that the lot-based allotment required post-hoc adjustment when Judah's allocation proved oversized. The lot determined territorial ownership, but the boundaries were set by human survey; the discrepancy required correction. This also confirms that the lot system was not mechanically applied without regard for demographic realities: the process included administrative flexibility.
10: Zebulun's allotment: the Galilee hill country south of the Nazareth ridge. Zebulun's territory is relatively small and landlocked (despite Genesis 49:13 suggesting a coastal orientation — 'Zebulun shall live by the seashore'). The gap between Jacob's blessing and the allotment is sometimes noted; the blessing may anticipate a later expansion toward the coast that never fully materialized. Isaiah 9:1-2 calls this territory 'Galilee of the Gentiles,' the region of darkness where a great light appears — applied in Matthew 4:13-16 to Jesus's Galilean ministry. Nazareth, Jesus's hometown, was in Zebulun's territory.
11: Zebulun's boundary runs to Sarid, then west. The specific location of Sarid is uncertain but was in the plain south of the Nazareth ridge — the transition zone between the Jezreel Valley and the Galilean highlands. Maralah and Dabbesheth appear only here; the unusual place names suggest authentic archival material from a survey document that preserved local names now otherwise lost.
12: Eastward from Sarid to Chisloth-tabor and Daberath — the boundary approaches Mount Tabor, the dominant peak of the lower Galilee region. Mount Tabor (Judges 4:6,12,14) was Deborah and Barak's staging point for the battle against Sisera — Zebulun's territory encompassed the command position for Israel's great Galilean victory against the Canaanite coalition.
13: From Gath-hepher to Eth-kazin and Rimmon — the northern Zebulun boundary. Gath-hepher is identified as the hometown of the prophet Jonah (2 Kings 14:25) — the prophet commanded to preach to Nineveh came from Zebulun, the most ethnically mixed region of Israel ('Galilee of the Gentiles'). The geographic connection between Jonah's Galilean background and his mission to the nations is narratively significant.
14: The boundary circles around to Hannathon and ends at the Valley of Iphtah-el. The 'turning' (sābab) of the boundary at the northern point follows the valley system; Hannathon appears in the el-Amarna letters (14th century BCE) as a Canaanite city under Egyptian administration — 'Hinnatuna' — confirming the archival depth of these place names.
15: Kattath, Nahalal, Shimron, Idalah, Bethlehem — twelve cities for Zebulun. This Bethlehem is the Galilean Bethlehem (Judges 17:7-9 refers to a Levite 'from Bethlehem in Judah' who stayed at 'Bethlehem in Zebulun') — a less famous namesake of the Judean Bethlehem. The existence of a northern Bethlehem ('house of bread') alongside the southern one reflects the common Semitic naming pattern for agricultural settlements.
16: Zebulun's allotment formula closes the section. The subsequent history of Zebulun in the book of Judges is one of the more active: Zebulun contributes troops to Deborah's battle (Judges 4:10; Joshua 5:14,18 — 'Zebulun is a people who risked their lives to the point of death'), and Elon the Zebulunite is a minor judge (Judges 12:11). The small allotment produced a consistently active tribe.
17: Issachar's allotment: the Jezreel Valley and its approaches. Issachar's territory is dominated by the Jezreel ('God sows') Valley, the most fertile agricultural plain in Canaan. Egypt maintained administrative control over the Jezreel throughout the Late Bronze Age (the el-Amarna letters show extensive Egyptian oversight of Megiddo, Beth-shean, and other Jezreel cities); the transition to Israelite administration was gradual and contested.
18: Jezreel, Chesulloth, Shunem — key sites. Jezreel gave its name to the valley; it became Ahab's summer residence (1 Kings 21:1) and the site of Naboth's vineyard (1 Kings 21:1-16), Jehu's coup (2 Kings 9:14-37), and Hosea's judgment oracle (Hosea 1:4-5). Shunem was the home of the 'great woman' whose son Elisha raised from the dead (2 Kings 4:8-37) and where Abishag the Shunammite came from (1 Kings 1:3). Issachar's cities were sites of some of the OT's most dramatic narratives.
19: Hapharaim, Shion, Anaharath — Jezreel valley sites. The valley floor was proverbially fertile; Issachar's territory was agriculturally rich but militarily exposed on all sides. Jacob's blessing suggests Issachar chose agricultural stability over independence: 'Issachar is a strong donkey... when he saw that his resting place was good and the land was pleasant, he bent his shoulder to bear the load and became a slave laborer' (Genesis 49:14-15) — an uncomplimentary assessment of a tribe that traded military autonomy for agricultural prosperity.
20: Rabbith, Kishion, Ebez — southern Issachar. These minor sites mark the transition from the Jezreel plain to the hill country; the southern boundary of Issachar is where the valley meets the Manassite hills, explaining the tension between the two tribes over specific valley cities (Joshua 17:10-11).
21: Remeth, En-gannim, En-haddah, Beth-pazzez — the eastern Issachar sites toward the Jordan valley. En-gannim ('spring of gardens') reflects the irrigation agriculture that made the eastern Jezreel Valley productive; garden-spring toponyms cluster in well-watered zones throughout the OT, marking the intersection of water and settlement.
22: The boundary reaches Tabor, Shahazumah, and Beth-shemesh (a northern Beth-shemesh, distinct from the Judean one), ending at the Jordan — sixteen cities for Issachar. Tabor appearing again (as a boundary marker shared with Zebulun) confirms that Mount Tabor served as the administrative landmark at the intersection of three tribal territories: Zebulun, Issachar, and Naphtali.
23: Issachar's allotment formula. The tribe's later prominence includes Tola son of Puah, an Issacharite judge (Judges 10:1-2), and Baasha king of Israel (1 Kings 15:27) — an Issacharite who seized the throne from Jeroboam's dynasty by assassination, suggesting that Issachar's agricultural wealth eventually translated into political ambition.
24: Asher's allotment: the western Galilee coastal strip, one of the most desirable territories in Canaan. The Phoenician coast was known for its timber (Lebanon cedar), purple dye (murex snails), and maritime trade. Asher's territory should have made it the wealthiest tribe; the failure notices (Judges 1:31-32) showing Asher living among the Canaanites rather than displacing them suggest the Asherites either lacked the military capacity or chose to coexist with the Phoenician urban populations rather than displace them.
25: Helkath, Hali, Beten, Achshaph — the inner Asher coast. Achshaph was a major Canaanite city-state whose king fought against Joshua in the northern campaign (Joshua 11:1; Joshua 12:20). Its inclusion in Asher's city list without comment on its continued Canaanite occupation anticipates Judges 1:31.
26: Alammelech, Amad, Mishal — the boundary approaches Carmel. Mount Carmel's promontory into the Mediterranean forms the major geographic feature of Asher's southern boundary; it separates the Asher coast (north) from the Manassite coast (south). Carmel's later fame as Elijah's confrontation site (1 Kgs 18) is rooted in its being the frontier between Asher, Manasseh, and the Jezreel.
27: The boundary turns to Beth-dagon, Zebulun, and the Valley of Iphtah-el. Beth-dagon ('house of Dagon') appears in both Asher's and Judah's allotments, reflecting the widespread distribution of Dagon-worship sites throughout Canaan. The Dagon-cult was not exclusively Philistine but was broadly Semitic; the place names preserve the religious geography of pre-Israelite Canaan.
28: Ebron, Rehob, Hammon, Kanah, and Greater Sidon. The appearance of Sidon in Asher's boundary reflects the ambitious scope of the allotment: Sidon was the principal Phoenician city-state, never incorporated into Israel. The text's inclusion of Sidon in the theoretical boundary while the Canaanites of the actual territory were never expelled (Judges 1:31) illustrates the persistent gap between ideal and actual possession.
29: The boundary returns through Ramah to Tyre and Hosah, ending at the sea near Aczib — twenty-two cities for Asher. Tyre, like Sidon, was never conquered; its inclusion marks the northern extent of the theoretical grant. The Tyre-Sidon coast later becomes the Syro-Phoenician region where Jesus heals the Canaanite woman's daughter (Matthew 15:21-28; Mark 7:24-30) — the land that Israel never possessed becomes the place where the Gentile mission begins.
30: Ummah, Aphek, Rehob — twenty-two cities total for Asher. Aphek on the Asher coast (distinct from the Jordan valley Aphek) was a Phoenician city; its appearance in the list confirms the theoretical scope. The failure to possess these coastal cities created the long-term Phoenician corridor separating the Israelite northern tribes from the sea — Israel remained essentially a land power throughout the monarchy because Asher never fulfilled its allotment.
31: Asher's allotment formula. The tribe's later history in Judges is conspicuously absent from the liberation narratives; Asher is criticized in Deborah's song for staying 'by the seashore, settling in its coves' (Judges 5:17) during the crisis against Sisera — the comfortable coastal tribe did not mobilize. Anna the prophetess (Luke 2:36-38) is from the tribe of Asher — the only Asherite named in the NT, living in Jerusalem and waiting for the consolation of Israel.
32: Naphtali's allotment: the upper Galilee and Sea of Galilee basin. This is the territory of the Galilee heartland — the region of Jesus's ministry. Capernaum, the base of Jesus's Galilean operations, was in Naphtali (Matthew 4:13 places it in 'the region of Zebulun and Naphtali'); Isaiah 9:1-2's promise of light in these two northern tribes is explicitly applied to Jesus's ministry.
33: Naphtali's boundary from Heleph to the oak of Zaanannim near Kedesh — the oak (ʾallôn) in the boundary description is the same landmark used in Deborah's narrative (Judges 4:11: 'near the oak of Zaanannim near Kedesh'). Heber the Kenite camped there; the exact landmark preserved in the allotment boundary reappears in the battle narrative of Judges 4, confirming the geographic precision of both texts.
34: The boundary connects Aznoth-tabor and runs to Hukkok, touching Zebulun to the south and Asher to the west, with Judah on the Jordan to the east. 'Judah on the Jordan' is textually disputed — Judah's territory doesn't extend to the upper Jordan; the text may reference a clan or district called 'Judah' in the upper Jordan area, or it may be a scribal error. The boundary description accurately defines Naphtali's triangular territory between the Sea of Galilee, upper Jordan, and the Galilean highlands.
35: Naphtali's fortified cities: Ziddim, Zer, Hammath, Rakkath, Kinnereth. Kinnereth gave its name to the Sea of Kinnereth (= Sea of Galilee); the city sat on the northwestern shore. Hammath ('hot springs') near Tiberias is attested by archaeological remains; its name reflects the geothermal springs along the western Sea of Galilee shore that made the region distinctive. Rakkath is identified with Tiberias by the Talmud — the city Herod Antipas founded and named for the Roman emperor.
36: Adamah, Ramah, Hazor — northern Naphtali. Hazor appears again (already destroyed in Joshua 11 and listed as a conquered city in Joshua 12); its inclusion in Naphtali's allotment confirms that the city's territory was assigned even though the city itself had been burned. Jabin king of Hazor (Judges 4:2) suggests either Canaanite reoccupation of the site or a separate ruler who inherited the Hazor name/title.
37: Kedesh, Edrei, En-hazor — the sites approaching the Syrian plateau. Kedesh in Galilee is the prominent northern city of refuge (Joshua 20:7) and the birthplace of Barak (Judges 4:6). The city's role as a refuge city and military muster point reflects its strategic position on the northern Galilean ridge.
38: Iron, Migdal-el, Horem, Beth-anath, Beth-shemesh — nineteen cities for Naphtali. Beth-anath ('house of Anat') preserves the name of the Canaanite warrior goddess; the failure notice in Judges 1:33 ('Naphtali did not drive out the inhabitants of Beth-shemesh or Beth-anath') explains how Anath's name survived in Israelite territory — they coexisted with those populations rather than eliminating them.
39: Naphtali's allotment formula. The tribe's subsequent history includes Barak (Judg 4-5), the Galilean leader of the anti-Sisera coalition, and a significant military contribution to Gideon's campaign (Judges 7:23). The later Isaiah passage (Isaiah 9:1-2) about light coming to Zebulun and Naphtali reflects their status as the most remote, most exposed, and most Gentile-adjacent tribes — the ones most in need of divine rescue.
40: Dan's allotment: the western foothill zone between Judah and Ephraim, including access to the coastal plain. Dan received prime real estate — the fertile Shephelah and the Aijalon Valley, major transit routes connecting the coastal plain to the hill country. The Samson narratives (Judg 13-16) are set precisely in this territory: Zorah, Eshtaol, Timnah, and Gaza are all Danite cities. The territory's strategic value made it intensely contested by the Philistines.
41: Zorah and Eshtaol — Samson's hometown and the site of Samson's burial (Judges 16:31). The 'camp of Dan between Zorah and Eshtaol' (Judges 13:25; Joshua 18:11-12) where the Spirit first stirred Samson locates the tribe's military assembly point precisely within this allotment. The allotment geography and the Samson narrative are geographically consistent throughout.
42: Ir-shemesh, Shaalabbin, Aijalon, Ithlah — the Aijalon Valley was the site of the Long Day battle (Joshua 10:12) and a major east-west transit corridor. Gezer (Joshua 19:45 below) controlled the valley's western exit; the entire Aijalon-Sorek system was the main approach from the coastal plain to the Judean hill country, explaining why this territory was contested throughout the biblical period.
43: Elon, Timnah, Ekron — the border with Philistine territory. Timnah is Samson's wife's hometown (Judges 14:1-2); the repeated Danite-Philistine conflict in Judges 13-16 reflects the geographic reality that Dan's territory directly abutted the Philistine pentapolis. The Philistine pressure on Dan was not incidental but structural — they shared a contested border.
44: Eltekeh, Gibbethon, Baalath — the coastal cities. Gibbethon is the Philistine city where Israelite kings Nadab (1 Kings 15:27) and Omri's predecessor Zimri were challenged during military campaigns (1 Kings 16:15-17) — the allotment city remained on the Philistine-Israelite frontier for centuries, a persistent flashpoint.
45: Jehud, Bene-berak, Gath-rimmon — the Philistine plain margin. Bene-berak ('sons of lightning') appears in Sennacherib's annals (ANET 287) as a city he conquered during the 701 BCE campaign — Assyrian administrative records confirm the city's existence and location in the coastal zone between Joppa and Ekron.
46: Me-jarkon and Rakkon with the territory facing Joppa. Joppa (modern Jaffa) is the natural harbor city of the central coast; its appearance as Danite territory reflects the allotment's theoretical claim to the coast. Dan never secured Joppa — the Philistines controlled the coast; Joppa only enters Israelite control under the Maccabees (1 Macc Joshua 12:33-34). Peter's vision at Joppa (Acts 10:9-16) is set in a city that was originally allotted to Dan but never possessed by Israel until the Hellenistic period.
47: 'But the Danites had difficulty taking possession of their territory, so they went up and attacked Leshem/Laish, took it... and settled there, calling it Dan.' This single verse summarizes the migration tradition told in detail in Judges 18. The Danite failure to possess their allotted coastal territory — driven out by the Philistines and Amorites — forced the tribe north, where they conquered an isolated Canaanite city. The original Danite territory became effectively absorbed into Philistine and then Judean control; 'Dan' as a northern city became a geographic and cultic landmark (Jeroboam's other golden calf was placed there, 1 Kings 12:29).
48: Dan's allotment formula for the original coastal territory. The tribe's split identity — southern allotment never possessed, northern conquest established as a second home — creates the 'from Dan to Beersheba' idiom of the monarchic period, where these two extremes define Israel's entire extent.
49: 'The Israelites gave Joshua son of Nun an inheritance among them.' The leader who spent seven years distributing the land to others finally receives his own portion — and he receives it last. The narrative structure is deliberate: Joshua's personal inheritance is withheld until every tribe has received theirs. The servant-leadership pattern — give first, receive last — is presented without commentary but is unmistakable in the narrative arrangement.
50: Timnath-serah in the Ephraimite hill country — Joshua's own tribe, his own people. The city is in the Ephraim highlands, away from the contested frontiers; Joshua retires to hill country he has to rebuild ('he rebuilt the city and settled there'). The act of rebuilding connects him to the patriarchal settlers who built altars and wells; Joshua's retirement is itself a form of possession, working the land he was given rather than merely receiving it as a title.
51: The closing formula for all the Cisjordan allotments: 'at the entrance of the tent of meeting at Shiloh, before the LORD.' The entire distribution process is framed as a liturgical act — the land is received in the presence of YHWH, mediated by priest (Eleazar), commander (Joshua), and tribal elders. The three parties who distributed the land represent the three pillars of Israelite communal life: worship, leadership, and family/clan structure. The formula closes the allotment chapters and marks the book's transition to the subsequent covenant chapters (Joshua 22-24).
